ffdc.title: FinnFoundDaChest
ffdc.long_description:
FinnFoundDaChest is a solo 2D adventure game inspired by the Adventure Time universe, built in plain Java (no engine).

The player controls Finn across a fixed map, finds keys, unlocks doors, and reaches the final chest as fast as possible (speedrun-oriented gameplay). I drew Finn's sprites myself and handled every game mechanic: movement (WASD/arrows), collisions, item management, debug mode (FPS, latency, coordinates), toggleable music, and an HTML README generated with Pandoc.

This was my first complete Java project outside of coursework, built by self-training on YouTube (RyiSnow tutorials). It taught me how to structure a Java project from scratch (package layout, asset management, game loop), how to persevere on a long solo project, and how to document code properly.

agile.title: Agile Welcome Project
agile.long_description:
Agile Welcome Project (2nd-year BUT): team of 4 building a terminal-playable Rogue-Like in Java over 10 weekly Scrum sprints on GitLab.

The game features multiple classes (warrior, mage...) with class-specific abilities, procedurally-placed floors, chests, equipment, a final boss, and an admin mode with cheat codes. Each sprint had its own spec, GitLab issues, retrospective, and playable increment.

This project gave me hands-on agile experience: dailies, planning poker, issue breakdown, merge requests, resolving Git conflicts in a 4-person team, and most importantly managing the gap between ambitious specs and real-world time constraints. The team coordination (task allocation, code reviews, mutual help on bugs) was as formative as the technical work itself.

sae33.title: SAE 3.3 Project
sae33.long_description:
SAE 3.3 (2nd-year BUT, team G3 "Mathieu Fanclub", 4 developers): a JavaFX application for visualizing and classifying data, implementing the k-NN (k-Nearest Neighbors) algorithm.

The app loads a dataset (Iris), plots it as a 2D scatter chart with user-chosen axes, lets the user add a point of unknown category, and determines its class via k-NN. The UI handles per-category coloring, multi-window graph drag-and-drop, and a generic CSV file loader.

My role: UI mockups, jalon-1 prototypes, coloring and classification system. Strict MVC architecture using JavaFX/FXML (SceneBuilder), JUnit tests, and full UML class diagrams. The project deepened my OOP (inheritance, generics), showed me MVC separation in real conditions, and let me build a supervised-learning algorithm end-to-end.

mte.title: MineToEarn
mte.description: Minecraft plugin + web interface, team project

corpsamer.title: Nuit de l'Info: CorpsAMer
corpsamer.description: HTML/CSS/JS webapp, Nuit de l'Info hackathon, team project

talkul.title: Talkul
talkul.long_description:
TalkUL is a web messaging app built in Java EE (Servlets, JSP, Tomcat) with PostgreSQL persistence, as part of the server-side web development module.

It handles authentication, conversation creation, message sending, and refreshes. Configuration is externalized via a `config.prop` file for the JDBC driver, database URL, and credentials, so secrets never end up hardcoded. Jackson is wired in for JSON/XML serialization, and Jakarta Migration handles adaptation to recent Servlet versions.

The project taught me the lifecycle of a classic Java webapp (WAR deployment on Tomcat, Servlets, context listeners), HTTP session handling on the server, configuration best practices (secrets outside of code), and how Servlets and JSP templates fit together before the age of modern frameworks.

woze.title: Woze
woze.description: Multi-modal JavaFX itinerary calculator, team project
woze.long_description:
Woze (SAE 2.01-2.02 "E3", 1st-year BUT, team of 3): a Java/JavaFX app that computes multi-modal itineraries between cities (train, bus, plane), optimizing for a user-chosen criterion (price, duration, or CO2e emissions).

The project was built in 3 successive versions:
- V1: shortest path, single-mode, in a graph built from CSV data
- V2: multi-modal with transfer costs between transport modes, exception handling, point-of-interest display
- V3: relative weighting of criteria and trip history

I worked on the OOP side (Voyage/Place/Platform model, CSV import, exception handling, threshold filtering) and the graph side (edge weighting, integrating transfers). MVC architecture with paper mockups then Figma prototype, FXML controllers, PMD checks. I learned to model a real-world problem as a graph, to evolve an architecture over 3 iterations without breaking it, and to collaborate on GitLab with version tags (POO-v2, GRAPHE-v2, IHM-v1).

maio.title: Ma.io
maio.description: Quirky multiplayer game (mayonnaise and aliens), Node.js + JS
maio.long_description:
Ma.io is a multiplayer web game (.io-like) built as a team for the JavaScript SAE, with a quirky theme: a mayonnaise battle against aliens.

Separate client/server architecture: a Node.js server (ES modules) owns shared game state (player positions, mayo stains, leaderboard), the client is plain JavaScript bundled with Webpack, and communication happens over WebSockets. The server broadcasts state to clients; each client sends inputs (movement, join/leave).

Challenges we overcame (from the team report): refactoring to cleanly split client and server after a monolithic start, setting up a TDD loop (notably on the Player class), calibrating physics/collision formulas through research and iterations, and writing "smart" bots (instead of ones wandering randomly). The game also stood out thanks to polished animations and a very strong team spirit — "no friction, everyone pulled their weight."

magare.title: MaGare
magare.description: Web reproduction of the SNCF app with real-time API
magare.long_description:
Ma Gare SNCF is a web reproduction of the SNCF mobile app "Ma Gare", deployed to production on GitHub Pages. A personal project driven by the desire to build a real, consumed app rather than yet another coursework exercise.

Stack: React 19, TypeScript, Vite 7, Tailwind CSS 4, DaisyUI 5, React Router 7, Atomic Design architecture (atoms / molecules / organisms / pages / services). Integrates the SNCF Navitia API for real-time departures and arrivals, station autocomplete, and trip detail (every stop).

Key features: side-by-side departures (blue) and arrivals (green) in desktop with a mobile toggle; a swipe-to-dismiss sheet for train details; destination filtering with favorites; date/time picker with scroll-wheel input; default station + favorites persisted in localStorage; light and dark themes using official SNCF colors; a delay bulletin encoded in LZ-String URLs.

CI/CD via GitHub Actions. Built end-to-end solo: design, API integration, architecture, deployment. Biggest takeaways: handling SPA routing on GitHub Pages correctly (404.html fallback) and designing mobile-first ergonomics.

weathersport.title: WeatherSport
weathersport.description: Weather + sports recommendations, Nextoo internship project
weathersport.long_description:
WeatherSport is one of the 3 apps I built during my internship at Nextoo (Roubaix, April-June 2025), as part of Nextoo Academy — an intensive Java 21 / Spring Boot 3.4 / Vue.js 3 / Docker training program.

The app matches weather data for a city with a sports catalog to recommend activities fitting the day's conditions (rain → indoor sports, good weather → outdoor sports, etc.). Spring Boot backend exposing a REST API, Vue.js frontend consuming the API, Docker containerization, GitLab CI/CD pipeline.

This was my first professional contact with Spring Boot, Vue 3 Composition API, and containerization. I discovered the rigor expected in a company setting: systematic code reviews, GitFlow, conventional commits, unit tests. The internship took me from academic to professional level in a few weeks and earned me a written recommendation from Nextoo's Deputy Managing Director for my rigor, autonomy, and curiosity.

nextmovie.title: NextMovie
nextmovie.description: Movie recommendation engine, Nextoo internship project
nextmovie.long_description:
NextMovie is the second app I built during my Nextoo internship: a movie recommendation engine that lets the user mark watched films, rate preferences, and get personalized suggestions.

Stack: Spring Boot 3.4 (Java 17), Spring WebFlux for calls to external movie APIs, Jakarta Bean Validation, Vue.js 3 + Vite on the frontend, PostgreSQL for persistence, Docker Compose to orchestrate db + back + front locally. Layered architecture (controllers / services / repositories / DTOs) following Spring best practices.

This project deepened my grasp of reactivity with WebFlux, async HTTP requests to third-party APIs, and the design of a clean relational schema with JPA/Hibernate. It also cemented my understanding of front/back split and the API contract between teams — which paid off on subsequent projects.

gds.title: GDS
gds.description: Stock management with REST API and dynamic interface, Nextoo internship
gds.long_description:
GDS (Gestion De Stock — Stock Management) is the third app I built near the end of my Nextoo internship: an internal inventory tool with a REST API, a dynamic interface, and reporting.

Same stack as the previous two (Spring Boot, Vue.js 3, PostgreSQL, Docker) but with heavier domain complexity: Product / Category / Stock / Movement entities, full CRUD endpoints, server-side filtering/sorting/pagination, a responsive UI for fast stock-in/out entries. Automated GitLab CI/CD workflow.

This final project is where I gained the most autonomy: I made the architectural decisions, chose the libraries, and prioritized features against the remaining time. It forced me to face realistic time estimation — I tended to underestimate frontend integration — and to break down tickets correctly. The intensive Nextoo training (3 full apps in 10 weeks) moved me up a level on rigor, advanced Git usage, and agile team practice.

dinnermatch.title: DinnerMatch
dinnermatch.description: Full-stack social platform to find dining partners. Node.js, React, PostgreSQL.
dinnermatch.long_description:
DinnerMatch is a full-stack social platform built during the university Startup Week (3rd-year BUT): a site connecting hotel guests to share themed dinners based on shared interests.

Stack: React 19 + TypeScript + Tailwind 4 + DaisyUI 5 frontend with a theme editor for hoteliers, a responsive calendar, and role-based dashboards (Client / Hotelier / Admin). Spring Boot 4 + Java 21 backend with Spring Security + JWT for auth, JPA/Hibernate, PostgreSQL 17, email sending via Scaleway TEM. GitLab CI/CD with coverage tracking and Docker (Nginx reverse proxy).

I worked backend-side on the API, JWT security, matching logic, and the admin CRUD panels. 18 sprints documented over 2 months, with a 5-person team (backend + frontend + infra). Project deployed to production at dinnermatch.fr.

Key lessons: coordinating a larger-than-usual team, aligning the API contract between front and back without friction, setting up a clean multi-profile environment (H2 in dev, PostgreSQL in prod), and delivering a usable MVP on a tight timeline.

refugegourmand.title: Le Refuge Gourmand
refugegourmand.description: Restaurant room booking website - Next.js, Prisma, NextAuth, Stripe.
refugegourmand.long_description:
Le Refuge Gourmand is an academic entrepreneurship project: a complete website for a fictional restaurant enabling online room booking.

Built with Next.js (static export deployed on GitHub Pages), the project integrates a modern and complete stack:
- Secure authentication with NextAuth and bcrypt hashing
- Online payment via Stripe
- Data management with Prisma ORM (PostgreSQL)
- Form validation with React Hook Form + Zod
- Responsive design with Tailwind CSS

The project follows Atomic Design and was an opportunity to explore a modern full-stack architecture in TypeScript.

weerdlebot.title: WeerdleBot
weerdlebot.description: Node.js bot that automatically plays Weerdle (Pokémon Wordle) with web control dashboard.
weerdlebot.long_description:
WeerdleBot is a personal bot that automates the game Weerdle, a Pokémon variant of the famous Wordle hosted on pokengine.com.

The bot automatically sends authenticated HTTP requests to submit word proposals, analyzes responses and adapts its strategy based on found or missed letters.

A web dashboard (Node.js + HTML/CSS/JS) allows starting/stopping the bot, displaying real-time logs via the terminal, and triggering VPN rotation (NordVPN) to avoid IP bans. Everything runs in a Docker container for maximum portability.

profile.hero: Full-stack developer apprentice at Mandarine Academy, studying Computer Science (BUT) at IUT de Lille. Passionate about building modern web applications - Java, Spring Boot, Vue.js, PHP/Symfony. I believe development isn't disappearing with AI - it's moving upward. I use AI as a tool to amplify my human skills.
profile.about:
I'm **Gaspard Catry**, a 20-year-old full-stack developer based in **Lille**. Currently in my 3rd year of **BUT Computer Science** at **IUT de Lille** (ranked **12/72** in S5, GPA **14.81/20**), doing my work-study at **Mandarine Academy** on a **PHP/Symfony/Tailwind** stack.

My internship at **Nextoo** (Roubaix) gave me intensive training in **Java 21 / Spring Boot 3.4 / Vue.js 3 / Docker** and hands-on experience building **3 internal web applications**. The Managing Director of Nextoo recommended me for my **rigor**, **autonomy** and **intellectual curiosity**.

Outside of work and studies, I build personal projects deployed in production: **MaGare** (web reproduction of the SNCF app). I also contribute to **open-source** projects on weekends.
profile.positioning:
Full-stack developer completing my **Computer Science degree (BUT)**, I specialize in building **robust, high-performance** web applications. My experience combines an intensive internship at **Nextoo** (**Java/Spring Boot/Vue.js**) and a work-study program at **Mandarine Academy** (**PHP/Symfony/Tailwind**).

In a world where AI is transforming software development, I position myself on what remains **fundamentally human**: understanding **business needs**, **architecting** complex systems, **debugging** critical production issues, and bearing the **technical responsibility** that AI cannot assume. I actively use AI as a **productivity lever** while maintaining the perspective needed to judge the quality and security of what it generates.

I am looking for a **full-stack developer** or **junior software engineer** position, ideally in a demanding technical team where I can continue to grow in **architecture** and **software quality**.
profile.reflexive:
My professional experiences taught me that I learn quickly and effectively when immersed in a demanding technical environment. At Nextoo, going from an academic level to a professional one in 10 weeks showed me I can ramp up rapidly on new technologies. At Mandarine Academy, working on a production application made me realize the importance of code rigor: every change has a real impact on users.

I also realized I am most effective when I understand the "why" behind tasks. The moments where I grew the most were when my supervisor took the time to explain architectural choices, not just "how to do it." This ability to understand business needs and translate them into technical solutions is precisely what makes the difference - and it's a deeply human skill.

The transition from academia to the professional world also taught me to manage my time differently: in a company, you need to estimate, prioritize and communicate on progress, not just code. I understood that client and team relationships, technical responsibility, and critical judgment are skills that AI does not replace. Tomorrow's developer isn't disappearing - they're moving upward, and those who thrive are the ones who know how to use AI as a tool rather than being subject to it.
profile.strengths:
Fast learning ability: confirmed by the intensive Nextoo training (3 complete projects in 10 weeks on an unknown stack) and the Deputy MD's recommendation.

Technical rigor: positive code reviews, habit of testing and documenting my code, respect for conventions (GitFlow, conventional commits, clean architecture).

Intellectual curiosity: I develop personal projects outside of work (DinnerMatch, MaGare, this portfolio), contribute to open-source on weekends, and constantly explore new technologies.

Versatility: ability to work on different stacks (Java/Spring, PHP/Symfony, React/TypeScript, Vue.js) and adapt quickly to varied technical environments.

AI as a tool: I integrate AI into my development workflow to accelerate code production, while maintaining the critical judgment needed to evaluate whether what it generates is good, secure, and fit for the context. I know when to trust it and when to take over - understanding business needs, architecting complex systems, debugging under production pressure, and bearing technical responsibility remain essential human skills.
profile.improvements:
Meeting communication: I tend to be reserved during initial team exchanges. I improved at Mandarine by actively participating in daily stand-ups, but this remains an area I continue to work on.

Time estimation: I often underestimated task duration at Nextoo, especially for frontend integration. I am learning to better break down tasks and add realistic buffers.

Stepping back: when focused on a technical problem, I tend to dive in too long before asking for help. I am learning to identify earlier when it is more efficient to consult a colleague.

Stress management: the first weeks in a professional environment were intense. I learned to better organize my work to reduce pressure, but managing tight deadlines remains a point of attention.
profile.aboutVision:
What remains **irreplaceable** in a developer is the ability to **understand business needs** and translate them into technical solutions, to **architect** complex systems, to take on **technical responsibility**, and to maintain **client/team relationships**. AI is a powerful tool I integrate into my daily workflow - **not a replacement, but an amplifier**.

My goal: become a **software engineer** who combines **technical expertise** with **human judgment** to build robust, performant web applications.

ghastrider.name: GhastRider
ghastrider.tagline: Dash mechanic for the Happy Ghast

overview.title: Overview
overview.content:
GhastRider adds a charge-and-dash mechanic to the Happy Ghast mount, inspired by the horse jump bar system.

Hold the dash key to charge, release to boost forward with a satisfying wind sound. Feed ice to your ghast for speed boosts!

Features

  • Charge bar — replaces the XP bar when riding a Happy Ghast
  • Minimum threshold — quick taps are ignored (2/18 bars)
  • Overcharge decay — holding too long reduces charge
  • Proportional cooldown — shorter charges mean shorter cooldowns
  • Auto-recharge — keep holding G through cooldown
  • Vertical boost — maintains altitude during dash
  • Ice feeding — feed ice for speed effects
  • Custom sounds — wind whoosh, ice crunch, happy purr

dash.title: Dash Mechanic
dash.content:
How to Dash

  1. Mount a Happy Ghast (equip it with a harness)
  2. Hold G to charge — the bar fills up
  3. Release G at the right moment for maximum power
  4. The bar shows the cooldown shrinking
  5. Once empty, dash again — or keep holding G to auto-recharge

Parameters

ParameterValue
Minimum charge2/18 bars (~11%)
Max charge time2 seconds
Max cooldown3 seconds
Vertical boost30%
KeyG (configurable)

ice.title: Ice Feeding
ice.content:
Feed ice to your Happy Ghast by right-clicking for temporary speed boosts.

ItemEffectDurationNotes
IceSpeed I10sDuration stacks
Packed IceSpeed II10sDuration stacks
Blue IceSpeed III10sDuration stacks
Powder Snow BucketSpeed I10sEmpties to bucket

Feeding a different ice type resets the duration to 10s with the new speed level.

Effects

  • Ice crunch sound
  • Happy ghast purr sound
  • Item particles of the block you fed

installation.title: Installation
installation.content:
Requirements

  • Minecraft 26.1.2
  • Fabric Loader or NeoForge
  • Fabric API (Fabric only)

Steps

  1. Install Fabric Loader or NeoForge for MC 26.1.2
  2. For Fabric: also install Fabric API
  3. Download the JAR from GitHub Releases
  4. Place the JAR in your mods/ folder
  5. Launch the game

commands.title: Commands
commands.content:
Debug Function

/function ghastrider:debug

Sets up a test environment:

  • Peaceful difficulty
  • Disables time, mob spawning, weather
  • Day + clear weather
  • Clears your inventory
  • Kills existing Happy Ghasts
  • Spawns 3 invulnerable Happy Ghasts
  • Gives: harness, all ice types, snowballs

Requires a Creative world with cheats enabled (Superflat recommended).

changelog.title: Changelog
changelog.content:
v26.1.2.1-alpha.1

April 16, 2026

First public release:

  • Dash mechanic with charge bar
  • Overcharge decay with minimum threshold
  • Proportional cooldown
  • Auto-recharge when holding G
  • Vertical boost during dash
  • Ice feeding with Speed effects
  • Custom sounds (wind, crunch, purr)
  • Item particles
  • Debug function
  • Fabric and NeoForge support for MC 26.1.2

overview.title: Overview
overview.content:
Numismatic Reimagined is a Minecraft currency mod inspired by Numismatic Overhaul, rebuilt from scratch.

It adds a full economy system with multiple coin denominations, shops, piggy banks, and villager trade integration.

This mod is currently in early development. Phase 1 (coin items) is complete.

currency.title: Currency System
currency.content:
Five denominations with a base-100 exchange rate:

CoinValueExchange
Bronze1Base unit
Silver100= 100 Bronze
Gold10,000= 100 Silver
Netherite1,000,000= 100 Gold
Star CoinUniqueAchievement reward

The Star Coin is a trophy earned by accumulating 1,000 Netherite Coins worth of transactions. Unique per player but not soulbound.

roadmap.title: Roadmap
roadmap.content:
The mod is being developed in 12 phases:

  1. Coin Items — Complete
  2. Purse System
  3. Piggy Bank Block
  4. Shop Block
  5. Configuration System
  6. Loot Tables + Mob Drops
  7. Villager Trades
  8. Marketplace + Commands
  9. Achievements + Star Coin
  10. Security Hardening
  11. Addon API
  12. NeoForge + MC 1.21.1 Port

overview.intro: GhastRider turns the Happy Ghast into a real aerial combat mount. Charge your dash and blast through the sky, feed your ghast ice to go even faster.
overview.screenshotRiding: Screenshot: flying on a Happy Ghast
overview.chargeBar: Charge bar — replaces the XP bar when riding a Happy Ghast
overview.minThreshold: Minimum threshold — quick taps are ignored (2/18 bars)
overview.overchargeDecay: Overcharge decay — holding too long reduces charge
overview.proportionalCd: Proportional cooldown — shorter charges mean shorter cooldowns
overview.autoRecharge: Auto-recharge — keep holding G through cooldown to recharge
overview.iceFeeding: Ice feeding — feed ice for speed effects
overview.customSounds: Custom sounds — wind whoosh on dash, ice crunch on feeding
overview.particles: Particles — item particles of the block you fed
overview.tip: Release at 80%+ charge for the best boost. But don't hold too long — charge decays after max!
overview.dashDesc: Hold G to charge, release to boost through the air. The longer you charge, the more powerful the boost.
overview.iceDesc: Feed ice to your ghast for temporary Speed effects. Blue Ice = Speed III!
overview.qs1: Equip a Happy Ghast with a harness and mount it
overview.qs2: You'll see the charge bar replacing the XP bar
overview.compatNote: Compatible with Fabric and NeoForge for Minecraft 26.1.2

dash.intro: When you ride a Happy Ghast, the XP bar is replaced by a charge bar. That's your dash.
dash.step1: Mount a Happy Ghast equipped with a harness
dash.step4: Wait for the cooldown (the bar empties) then go again
dash.overchargeTip: Overcharge starts when the bar is full. Charge slowly drops to the minimum threshold (2/18). Release before to keep more power!
dash.tipRelease: Release when the bar is almost full for the best boost. No need to wait for 100%.
dash.tipAutoRecharge: Keep holding G even during cooldown — the dash will auto-recharge as soon as possible.
dash.tipOvercharge: Warning: if you hold too long after the bar is full, the charge drops back down. Release at the right moment!

ice.intro: Feed ice to your Happy Ghast (right-click while riding) to get temporary speed boosts.
ice.resetWarning: Feeding a different ice type resets the duration to 10s with the new speed level. For example, if you feed Blue Ice (Speed III) then regular Ice (Speed I), you lose Speed III.
ice.screenshotFeeding: Screenshot: feeding ice to a Happy Ghast
ice.itemParticles: Item particles of the ice block fed
ice.howToDesc: Hold an ice block and right-click your Happy Ghast while riding it. The speed effect lasts 10 seconds and can be stacked by feeding the same ice type multiple times.

install.step1: Install Fabric Loader or NeoForge for MC 26.1.2
install.step2: For Fabric: also install Fabric API
install.step3: Download the JAR from GitHub Releases

commands.debugDesc: Runs a command that sets up a test world to try the mod quickly:
commands.requiresCreative: Requires a Creative world with cheats enabled (Superflat recommended).

items.loaderSupport: Fabric and NeoForge support for MC 26.1.2

config.intro: GhastRider is configurable through Minecraft's options. Here's what you can change.
config.keybindDesc: By default, the dash key is G. You can change it in the game options:
config.keybindStep2: Go to Options > Controls > Key Binds
config.keybindStep3: Look for "GhastRider Dash" in the GhastRider category
config.keybindStep4: Click on it and press your new desired key
config.screenshotKeybind: Screenshot: controls menu showing the GhastRider Dash keybind
config.settDashKeyDesc: The key to charge and activate the dash
config.moreSettingsNote: More configuration options (charge time, cooldown, etc.) will be added in a future update.
